skill.name: scaffold-exercises
skill.description: Create course exercise directory structures with sections, variants, readme stubs, lint checks, and git-aware moves.
skill.summary: Scaffold course exercises that follow expected section, variant, readme, and linting rules.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found Markdown inline code and placeholder text rather than executable Ruby or cryptographic code. The skill does instruct the agent to create directories, write files, run local lint commands, use git moves, and commit changes, so it carries a moderate operational risk that requires user review.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell and Git Workflow Requires Supervision
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run local commands such as linting, mkdir, git mv, and git commit. These commands are legitimate for the stated scaffolding purpose, but they can modify the working tree and create commits, so users should review changes before execution and publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly documented and match the skill purpose. Risk comes from local file and git side effects, not from evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Finding Is a Markdown False Positive
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged inline backticks as Ruby shell execution. The reviewed locations are Markdown examples and command names, not Ruby code or dynamic shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is Markdown, and the flagged backticks delimit inline code or fenced examples. No Ruby source or command interpolation is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is a Text Match False Positive
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography at lines containing the frontmatter description and placeholder readme text. No cryptographic algorithm or security-sensitive hashing code appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose and a stub description example. I found no evidence of DES, MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic implementation.

content.value_statement: Course authors need consistent exercise folders that pass project lint rules. This skill turns a section plan into scaffolded problem, solution, and explainer folders with required readmes.
actual_capabilities: Parses section and exercise plans into numbered directory names.
Creates problem, solution, and explainer variant folders.
Adds required readme stubs with titles and descriptions.
Runs the project lint command described by the skill.
Uses git-aware moves when renaming exercise directories.
Checks common lint requirements before finishing.
limitations: It is tailored to repositories that use the documented exercise layout.
It does not design full lesson content beyond basic stubs.
It depends on the local lint command being installed.
It can modify files and git state, so review is required.

use_cases.title: Create a New Course Section
use_cases.description: Convert a list of lessons into correctly numbered exercise folders with starter readmes.

use_cases.title: Add Problem and Solution Variants
use_cases.description: Build matching student and reference folders for an existing exercise plan.

use_cases.title: Renumber Existing Exercises
use_cases.description: Move exercise folders with git history preserved and rerun lint checks.

prompt_templates.title: Scaffold One Exercise
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single explainer exercise.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create exercise 02.01 named Prompt Basics in section 02 Prompt Engineering. Use the default explainer variant.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Section From a Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Create several exercises with default variants.
prompt_templates.prompt: Scaffold section 04 Retrieval Practice with exercises 04.01 Search Basics, 04.02 Ranking Results, and 04.03 Evaluation Metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Add Multiple Variants
prompt_templates.scenario: Create problem, solution, and explainer folders.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create exercise 05.02 Memory Stores in section 05 Agent Memory with explainer, problem, and solution variants.

prompt_templates.title: Move and Validate Exercises
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced maintenance workflow with git-aware renaming.
prompt_templates.prompt: Renumber 03.04 Tool Selection to 03.05 Tool Selection, preserve git history, update ordering, and run the exercise lint check.

output_examples.input: Create section 05 Memory Skill Building with three explainer exercises.
output: A new section folder is created with the correct numeric prefix.
Each exercise gets an explainer folder and readme stub.
The lint command is run and any reported scaffold issues are fixed.

output_examples.input: Add problem and solution variants to an existing exercise.
output: The exercise gains problem and solution folders.
Each variant receives a non-empty readme.
The final structure is checked against the documented lint rules.

best_practices: Provide exact section numbers, exercise numbers, and desired variants.
Review generated readmes before committing the scaffold.
Run lint after every create, move, or rename operation.
anti_patterns: Do not use it for repositories with unrelated directory conventions.
Do not skip review when git commit is requested.
Do not rely on stub readmes as final teaching content.

faq.question: What repository structure does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It expects an exercises folder with numbered sections and numbered exercise folders.

faq.question: Can it create problem and solution folders?
faq.answer: Yes. Ask for problem, solution, explainer, or a combination of those variants.

faq.question: Does it write complete lessons?
faq.answer: No. It creates scaffold files and minimal readme content for later editing.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow includes local lint, mkdir, git mv, and optional git commit commands.

faq.question: Is it safe for any project?
faq.answer: No. It is safest in repositories that already use the documented exercise layout.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing changes?
faq.answer: Review created folders, readme text, lint results, and any git commit before sharing.
